title: Docker: The Complete A–Z Guide
description: A comprehensive end-to-end guide to Docker from fundamentals to production. Covers installation, images and containers, Dockerfiles, multi-stage builds, volumes and data persistence, networking, Docker Compose for multi-container apps, security hardening, image scanning, logging and monitoring, CI/CD integration, and production best practices — for beginners and experienced developers alike.
author: E-Learning Platform
heroAlt: Docker architecture diagram showing Docker Engine, the Build → Ship → Run pipeline with multi-stage builds and registries, Docker Compose multi-service stack with Nginx, API, PostgreSQL and Redis, and production features including volumes, networking, security hardening, health checks, resource limits, image scanning, and logging

sections.title: 1. Introduction
sections.description: What Docker is, the problem it solves, and what this guide covers.
content: Docker is the industry standard for building, shipping, and running applications in containers. It packages your application with all its dependencies — code, runtime, system libraries, configuration files — into a lightweight, portable unit called a container. This container runs consistently on any system that has Docker installed, whether it's your laptop, a test server, or a production cluster in the cloud.

The core problem Docker solves is the 'works on my machine' syndrome. Without containers, differences between development and production environments cause subtle bugs and deployment failures. Docker eliminates this by providing a consistent environment across the entire software delivery pipeline.

This guide covers everything from your first Docker install to production-ready multi-container deployments. You will learn containers and images, Dockerfiles and multi-stage builds, volumes and networking, Docker Compose, security hardening, image scanning, CI/CD integration, and battle-tested production patterns.

infoBox.content: This guide is tested against Docker Engine 29.x and Docker Compose v2.40+, which are the current standard releases as of mid-2026. BuildKit is the default build backend, and the legacy docker-compose (Python) binary is fully retired.

cards.title: What You Will Learn
cards.desc: Images and container lifecycle, Dockerfile optimization, multi-stage builds, volumes and networking, Docker Compose, production hardening, image scanning, CI/CD pipelines, and troubleshooting.

cards.title: Prerequisites
cards.desc: Basic command-line familiarity, a Linux VM or machine with sudo access (or macOS/Windows with Docker Desktop), and an internet connection.

sections.title: 2. Core Concepts: Images, Containers, and the Docker Engine
sections.description: Understanding the fundamental building blocks of Docker.
content: Before writing a single command, it is essential to understand the three core components: images, containers, and the Docker Engine.

An image is a read-only template that contains everything needed to run an application — a base operating system, application code, dependencies, environment variables, and configuration. Images are built from a Dockerfile using the docker build command. They are stored in a registry (like Docker Hub) and can be versioned with tags.

A container is a runnable instance of an image. When you run an image, Docker adds a thin writable layer on top of the image layers and starts the application process inside an isolated environment. Containers are ephemeral by design — they can be stopped, destroyed, and recreated with minimal overhead.

The Docker Engine is the runtime that builds and runs containers. It consists of a daemon (dockerd) that manages containers, images, networks, and volumes, and a CLI (docker) that communicates with the daemon via a REST API.
Registries store and distribute images. Docker Hub is the default public registry, but you can use private registries like AWS ECR, GitHub Container Registry (ghcr.io), or a self-hosted registry.

rows: Running process with its own filesystem and network

items.label: Docker Architecture Diagram
content: │                    Docker Client (CLI)                 │
│  docker build, docker run, docker pull, docker push   │
│ REST API (Unix socket / TCP)
│                   Docker Daemon (dockerd)               │
│  │ Container │  │  Image   │  │  Volume  │             │
│  │   nginx   │  │ postgres │  │ pg_data  │             │
│  │ Container │  │  Network │  │  Build   │             │
│  │   myapp   │  │  bridge  │  │  Cache   │             │
│                 Registries (Docker Hub, ECR, ghcr.io)    │
│              Store and distribute container images      │

sections.title: 3. Installing Docker
sections.description: Installing Docker Engine on Linux or Docker Desktop on macOS/Windows.
content: Docker Engine is available on Linux, macOS, and Windows. On Linux, you install the Docker Engine daemon directly. On macOS and Windows, Docker Desktop provides a convenient GUI along with the CLI tools.
For production servers, always use Docker Engine on Linux. Docker Desktop is designed for development workstations.

items.label: Install on Ubuntu/Debian (Production Recommended)
items.code:
user@host:~$ sudo apt-get update
user@host:~$ sudo apt-get install -y ca-certificates curl
user@host:~$ sudo install -m 0755 -d /etc/apt/keyrings
user@host:~$ sudo curl -fsSL https://download.docker.com/linux/ubuntu/gpg -o /etc/apt/keyrings/docker.asc
user@host:~$ sudo chmod a+r /etc/apt/keyrings/docker.asc

user@host:~$ echo "deb [arch=$(dpkg --print-architecture) signed-by=/etc/apt/keyrings/docker.asc] https://download.docker.com/linux/ubuntu $(. /etc/os-release && echo "$VERSION_CODENAME") stable" | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/docker.list > /dev/null

user@host:~$ sudo apt-get update
user@host:~$ sudo apt-get install -y docker-ce docker-ce-cli containerd.io docker-buildx-plugin docker-compose-plugin

user@host:~$ sudo systemctl enable docker
user@host:~$ sudo systemctl start docker

items.label: Verify Installation
items.code:
user@host:~$ sudo docker run hello-world

Hello from Docker!
This message shows that your installation appears to be working correctly.

To generate this message, Docker took the following steps:
 1. The Docker client contacted the Docker daemon.
 2. The Docker daemon pulled the "hello-world" image from the Docker Hub.
 3. The Docker daemon created a new container from that image which runs the
    executable that produces the output you are currently reading.
 4. The Docker daemon streamed that output to the Docker client, which sent it
    to your terminal.

items.label: Post-Install: Run Docker Without sudo
items.code:
user@host:~$ sudo usermod -aG docker $USER
user@host:~$ newgrp docker
user@host:~$ docker run hello-world
# Should work without sudo

items.label: Install on RHEL/CentOS/Fedora
items.code:
user@host:~$ sudo dnf config-manager --add-repo https://download.docker.com/linux/rhel/docker-ce.repo
user@host:~$ sudo dnf install -y docker-ce docker-ce-cli containerd.io docker-buildx-plugin docker-compose-plugin
user@host:~$ sudo systemctl enable --now docker

items.label: Install on macOS (Docker Desktop)
items.text: Download Docker Desktop for Mac from https://docs.docker.com/desktop/setup/install/mac-install/ and install the .dmg. Docker Desktop includes Docker Engine, Docker CLI, Docker Compose, and BuildKit in a single package with a system tray menu.
items.contentAfter: After installation, verify with: docker --version && docker compose version

items.label: Install on Windows (Docker Desktop)
items.text:
Download Docker Desktop for Windows from https://docs.docker.com/desktop/setup/install/windows-install/ and install the .exe. Docker Desktop requires WSL 2 (Windows Subsystem for Linux) on Windows 10/11.

Enable WSL 2 before installing: wsl --install

Then install Docker Desktop, which automatically integrates with your WSL 2 distribution.

items.label: Verify Docker Engine and Compose Versions
items.code:
user@host:~$ docker --version
Docker version 29.4.2, build abc1234

user@host:~$ docker compose version
Docker Compose version v2.40.0

user@host:~$ docker info
Client:
 Version:    29.4.2
 Context:    default

Server:
 Engine Version:  29.4.2
 OSType:          linux
 Architecture:    x86_64
 CPUs:            4
 Total Memory:    7.78GiB
 Containers:      2
 Running:         1
 Paused:          0
 Stopped:         1
 Images:          8
 Server Version:  29.4.2

sections.title: 4. Running Your First Container
sections.description: Essential Docker commands for managing containers and images.
content: With Docker installed, let's run containers, inspect them, execute commands inside them, and clean them up. These commands form the foundation of daily Docker work.

rows: Create and start a container from an image
Map host port to container port
Assign a name to the container
Follow log output in real time
Execute a command inside a running container

items.label: Run an Nginx Web Server
items.code:
user@host:~$ docker run -d --name my-nginx -p 8080:80 nginx:alpine
Unable to find image 'nginx:alpine' locally
alpine: Pulling from library/nginx
5c1f6215d1b8: Pull complete
c3b4d5e6f7a8: Pull complete
digest: sha256:abc123...
Status: Downloaded newer image for nginx:alpine
abc12345def67890abcdef1234567890abcdef1234567890abcdef1234567890ab

user@host:~$ docker ps
CONTAINER ID   IMAGE          COMMAND                  PORTS                  STATUS        NAMES
abc12345def6   nginx:alpine   "/docker-entrypoint.…"   0.0.0.0:8080->80/tcp   Up 2 minutes  my-nginx

user@host:~$ curl -s http://localhost:8080 | head -n 5
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
<title>Welcome to nginx!</title>
<style>
html { color-scheme: light dark; }

items.label: Inspect and Debug a Container
items.code:
user@host:~$ docker logs my-nginx
/docker-entrypoint.sh: /docker-entrypoint.d/ is not empty, will attempt to perform configuration
/docker-entrypoint.sh: Looking for shell scripts...
/docker-entrypoint.sh: Launching...
2026/07/06 10:00:00 [notice] 1#1: start worker processes

user@host:~$ docker exec -it my-nginx sh
/ # ls -la /usr/share/nginx/html/
drwxr-xr-x   2 root root   4096 Jul  6 10:00 .
drwxr-xr-x   3 root root   4096 Jul  6 10:00 ..
-rw-r--r--   1 root root    497 Jul  6 10:00 50x.html
-rw-r--r--   1 root root    615 Jul  6 10:00 index.html
/ # exit

user@host:~$ docker inspect my-nginx --format '{{.NetworkSettings.IPAddress}}'
172.17.0.2

user@host:~$ docker inspect my-nginx --format '{{.State.Status}}'
running

items.label: Clean Up Containers and Images
items.code:
user@host:~$ docker stop my-nginx
my-nginx

user@host:~$ docker rm my-nginx
my-nginx

user@host:~$ docker rm -f my-nginx  # If still running
my-nginx

# Remove unused resources:
user@host:~$ docker container prune
WARNING! This will remove all stopped containers.
Are you sure you want to continue? [y/N] y
Deleted Containers:
abc12345def6

user@host:~$ docker image prune
WARNING! This will remove all dangling images.
Are you sure you want to continue? [y/N] y

user@host:~$ docker system prune -a  # Remove ALL unused containers, images, networks
WARNING! This will remove all unused resources.
Are you sure you want to continue? [y/N] y

Total reclaimed space: 342.5MB

sections.title: 5. Dockerfile Fundamentals
sections.description: Writing your first Dockerfile and understanding the key instructions.
content: A Dockerfile is a text file with instructions that define how to build a Docker image. Each instruction creates a layer in the image. Understanding the core instructions — FROM, RUN, COPY, CMD, ENV, WORKDIR, and EXPOSE — covers 90% of use cases.

infoBox.content: Always pin base image versions with exact tags (e.g., node:22-alpine, python:3.12-slim). Never use :latest in production — it produces non-reproducible builds.

rows: Copy files from host to image

items.label: Example: Node.js Dockerfile
items.code:
# Dockerfile for a Node.js application
FROM node:22-alpine AS base

WORKDIR /app

# Copy dependency files first (for better caching)
COPY package.json package-lock.json ./

# Install production dependencies only
RUN npm ci --only=production

# Copy application source
COPY . .

# Application listens on this port
EXPOSE 3000

# Run as non-root user
USER node

# Start the application
CMD ["node", "server.js"]

items.label: Example: Python (Flask/FastAPI) Dockerfile
items.code:
# Dockerfile for a Python web application
FROM python:3.12-slim

WORKDIR /app

# Install system dependencies
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
    gcc \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Copy requirements and install Python dependencies
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# Copy application code
COPY . .

EXPOSE 8000

# Run with uvicorn (FastAPI) or gunicorn (Flask)
CMD ["uvicorn", "main:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000"]

items.label: Example: Go Dockerfile (Compiled Binary)
items.code:
# Dockerfile for a Go application
FROM golang:1.23-alpine AS builder

WORKDIR /build

COPY go.mod go.sum ./
RUN go mod download

COPY . .
RUN CGO_ENABLED=0 GOOS=linux go build -o /app ./cmd/server

# Minimal runtime image
FROM alpine:3.20

RUN apk add --no-cache ca-certificates tzdata

COPY --from=builder /app /app

EXPOSE 8080

CMD ["/app"]

items.label: The .dockerignore File
content: The .dockerignore file excludes files from the build context, similar to .gitignore. This speeds up builds and prevents secrets from being copied into the image.
Place .dockerignore in the same directory as your Dockerfile.
items.code:
# .dockerignore
node_modules
npm-debug.log
.git
.gitignore
.env
.env.*
Dockerfile
.dockerignore
*.md
dist
.cache
coverage
test
__pycashe__
*.pyc

sections.title: 6. Building Images with docker build
sections.description: Building, tagging, and managing Docker images.
content: The docker build command reads a Dockerfile and produces an image. Understanding the build context, tagging conventions, and BuildKit features is essential for efficient image builds.

rows: Build an image with a name and tag
Stop at a specific multi-stage build stage
Tag an image with a new name
Download an image from a registry
Upload an image to a registry

items.label: Build and Tag an Image
items.code:
user@host:~/myapp$ docker build -t myapp:1.0.0 .
[+] Building 12.5s (10/10) FINISHED
 => [internal] load build definition from Dockerfile                   0.1s
 => [internal] load metadata for docker.io/library/node:22-alpine      1.2s
 => [internal] load .dockerignore                                      0.1s
 => [1/5] FROM node:22-alpine@sha256:abc...                            1.5s
 => [2/5] WORKDIR /app                                                 0.1s
 => [3/5] COPY package*.json ./                                        0.1s
 => [4/5] RUN npm ci --only=production                                 6.8s
 => [5/5] COPY . .                                                     0.3s
 => exporting to image                                                 0.5s
 => => naming to docker.io/library/myapp:1.0.0

user@host:~/myapp$ docker images
REPOSITORY   TAG       IMAGE ID       CREATED          SIZE
myapp        1.0.0     abc12345def6   12 seconds ago   182MB
nginx        alpine    def67890abc1   2 weeks ago      42.6MB

items.label: Understanding the Build Context
items.text:
The final argument to docker build (the . in the command) specifies the build context — the directory sent to the Docker daemon. Everything in the context is available to COPY instructions in the Dockerfile.

A large build context slows down builds. Keep it small by:
1. Using a .dockerignore file to exclude unnecessary files
2. Placing the Dockerfile at the project root (not in a subdirectory that requires sending extra files)
3. Being intentional about what goes in the context — you don't need node_modules, .git, or test data

items.label: BuildKit Features (Default in Docker 29+)
text: BuildKit is the default build backend in Docker Engine 29+. It provides significant improvements over the legacy builder:
- Parallel stage execution — multi-stage build stages run concurrently when they don't depend on each other
- Content-addressable caching — cache keys are based on content, not just command strings
--mount=type=cache — cache package manager downloads across builds
--mount=type=secret — securely mount secrets during build without embedding them in layers
--mount=type=ssh — forward SSH agent for cloning private repositories
Automatic garbage collection of unused cache data

items.label: Using BuildKit Cache Mounts
items.code:
# Dockerfile with BuildKit cache mounts
# Cache apt and pip packages across builds
FROM python:3.12-slim

WORKDIR /app

# Cache apt packages
RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/var/cache/apt,sharing=locked \
    --mount=type=cache,target=/var/lib/apt/lists,sharing=locked \
    apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends gcc

COPY requirements.txt .

# Cache pip packages
RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/root/.cache/pip \
    pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

COPY . .

CMD ["python", "app.py"]

sections.title: 7. Layer Optimization and the Build Cache
sections.description: Understanding Docker image layers and maximizing cache efficiency.
content: Every instruction in a Dockerfile creates a layer. Layers are cached — if a layer hasn't changed, Docker reuses it from the cache instead of rebuilding it. Understanding this cache mechanism is critical for fast builds.

infoBox.content: The golden rule of Dockerfile layer ordering: put instructions that change least often at the top, and instructions that change most often at the bottom. This maximizes cache reuse.

items.label: Layer Ordering — Good vs Bad
items.code:
# BAD: Source code copied before dependencies
# Changing any source file invalidates the npm install cache
FROM node:22-alpine
WORKDIR /app
COPY . .                          # Changes on every code edit
RUN npm ci --only=production      # Re-runs on every build even if package.json hasn't changed
CMD ["node", "server.js"]

# GOOD: Dependencies installed before copying source
# npm ci only re-runs when package files change
FROM node:22-alpine
WORKDIR /app
COPY package.json package-lock.json ./   # Rarely changes
RUN npm ci --only=production             # Cached unless package files change
COPY . .                                 # Changes on every code edit, but this is fast
CMD ["node", "server.js"]

items.label: Checking Layer Cache Status
items.code:
user@host:~/myapp$ docker build -t myapp .
[+] Building 15.2s (10/10) FINISHED

# Add a small change to source code, rebuild:
user@host:~/myapp$ docker build -t myapp .
[+] Building 1.8s (10/10) FINISHED
 => [internal] load build definition from Dockerfile         0.1s
 => CACHED [2/5] WORKDIR /app                               0.0s
 => CACHED [3/5] COPY package*.json ./                      0.0s
 => CACHED [4/5] RUN npm ci --only=production               0.0s
 => [5/5] COPY . .                                          0.8s  # Only this re-runs!
 => exporting to image                                      0.3s

rows: Removes build tools from final image
Builder stage with Go SDK, runtime stage with compiled binary only
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install ... && cleanup
Use npm ci instead of npm install

sections.title: 8. Multi-Stage Builds
sections.description: Building smaller, more secure images by separating build and runtime environments.
content: Multi-stage builds are the single most impactful optimization in modern Docker. The pattern uses multiple FROM statements in one Dockerfile. The first stage compiles or bundles the application with all build tools. The final stage copies only the runtime artifacts — no compilers, no dev dependencies, no intermediate files.
The result: smaller images (often 80-90% smaller), fewer CVEs (fewer packages in the final image), and faster deployments.

infoBox.content: Multi-stage builds are not optional for production images. Every official Docker best practice guide, from Docker Inc. to the CNCF, mandates multi-stage builds for production containers.

items.label: Node.js Multi-Stage Build
items.code:
# Stage 1: Install dependencies and build
FROM node:22-alpine AS builder

WORKDIR /build

COPY package.json package-lock.json ./
RUN npm ci

COPY . .
RUN npm run build

# Stage 2: Production runtime (no dev tools, no source code)
FROM node:22-alpine AS production

WORKDIR /app

# Copy only production dependencies
COPY package.json package-lock.json ./
RUN npm ci --only=production && npm cache clean --force

# Copy compiled output from builder
COPY --from=builder /build/dist ./dist

EXPOSE 3000

USER node

CMD ["node", "dist/server.js"]

# Build output:
# Standard build:  ~1.2GB (includes devDependencies, TypeScript compiler, source maps)
# Multi-stage:      ~180MB (only production code and runtime)

items.label: Python Multi-Stage Build
items.code:
# Stage 1: Builder
FROM python:3.12-slim AS builder

WORKDIR /build

COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir --user -r requirements.txt

COPY . .

# Stage 2: Runtime
FROM python:3.12-slim

WORKDIR /app

# Copy installed packages from builder
COPY --from=builder /root/.local /root/.local

# Make sure scripts in .local are usable
ENV PATH=/root/.local/bin:$PATH

COPY --from=builder /build/app ./app

EXPOSE 8000

USER 10001

CMD ["uvicorn", "app.main:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000"]

items.label: Go Multi-Stage Build (Most Dramatic Size Reduction)
items.code:
# Stage 1: Build the Go binary
FROM golang:1.23-alpine AS builder

WORKDIR /build

COPY go.mod go.sum ./
RUN go mod download

COPY . .

# Build a statically linked binary
RUN CGO_ENABLED=0 GOOS=linux go build -a -ldflags '-s -w' -o /app ./cmd/server

# Stage 2: Minimal runtime
FROM scratch

COPY --from=builder /app /app

EXPOSE 8080

ENTRYPOINT ["/app"]

# Build output:
# Standard golang image: ~950MB
# Multi-stage with scratch: ~15MB (just the compiled binary!)

items.label: Multi-Stage Build Tips
text: 1. Name your stages (FROM node:22-alpine AS builder) — this makes COPY --from=builder readable
2. Use docker build --target STAGE to stop at a specific stage for debugging
3. BuildKit runs independent stages in parallel automatically
4. The final stage should be as minimal as possible — use scratch, distroless, or alpine
5. Never include build tools, package managers, or dev dependencies in the final stage

sections.title: 9. Volumes and Data Persistence
sections.description: Managing persistent data with named volumes, bind mounts, and tmpfs.
content: Containers are ephemeral — when you remove a container, all data written to its writable layer is lost. Volumes provide persistent storage that survives container lifecycle events. Docker supports three types of mounts: named volumes (managed by Docker), bind mounts (host directory mounted into the container), and tmpfs mounts (in-memory storage).

infoBox.content: Never store persistent data in a container's writable layer. Use named volumes for production databases. Bind mounts are convenient for development but can have permission issues and are harder to back up.

items.label: Named Volume Commands
items.code:
# Create a named volume
docker volume create pg_data

# List volumes
docker volume ls
DRIVER    VOLUME NAME
local     pg_data

# Inspect a volume
docker volume inspect pg_data
[
    {
        "CreatedAt": "2026-07-06T10:00:00Z",
        "Driver": "local",
        "Mountpoint": "/var/lib/docker/volumes/pg_data/_data",
        "Name": "pg_data",
        "Options": null,
        "Scope": "local"
    }
]

# Remove a volume
docker volume rm pg_data

# Remove all unused volumes
docker volume prune

items.label: Using Volumes with PostgreSQL
items.code:
# Run PostgreSQL with a named volume for data persistence
docker run -d \
    --name postgres \
    -e POSTGRES_USER=myapp \
    -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=secret \
    -e POSTGRES_DB=mydb \
    -v pg_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data \
    -p 5432:5432 \
    postgres:16-alpine

# Data survives container removal:
docker stop postgres && docker rm postgres
docker run -d \
    --name postgres \
    -e POSTGRES_USER=myapp \
    -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=secret \
    -e POSTGRES_DB=mydb \
    -v pg_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data \
    postgres:16-alpine
# All data is still there!

items.label: Bind Mounts for Development
items.code:
# Mount current directory into container for live code reloading
docker run -d \
    --name myapp-dev \
    -v $(pwd):/app \
    -p 3000:3000 \
    node:22-alpine \
    npx nodemon server.js

# Read-only bind mount for configuration files:
docker run -d \
    --name nginx \
    -v /host/path/nginx.conf:/etc/nginx/nginx.conf:ro \
    -p 80:80 \
    nginx:alpine

items.label: Backing Up a Volume
items.code:
# Backup a named volume to a tar file
docker run --rm -v pg_data:/source -v $(pwd):/backup alpine \
    tar czf /backup/pg_data_backup.tar.gz -C /source .

# Restore a volume from a tar file
docker run --rm -v pg_data:/target -v $(pwd):/backup alpine \
    tar xzf /backup/pg_data_backup.tar.gz -C /target

sections.title: 10. Docker Networking
sections.description: Understanding Docker network drivers and container communication.
content: Docker networking enables containers to communicate with each other and with the outside world. Docker provides several built-in network drivers, each designed for different use cases. Understanding when to use each driver is critical for both development and production deployments.

rows: IP only (default), names on custom bridges
Performance-critical, container uses host network stack
Containers with own MAC addresses, legacy apps

items.label: User-Defined Bridge Networks (Recommended)
content: The default bridge network has a critical limitation: containers on it can only communicate by IP address, not by name. IP addresses are assigned dynamically — restarting a container changes its IP.

A user-defined bridge network fixes this. Docker runs an embedded DNS server on user-defined networks, so containers can reach each other by container name. This is the pattern every Docker Compose setup uses.
Always create a user-defined network for multi-container applications.
items.code:
# Create a user-defined bridge network
docker network create myapp-network

# Run two containers on the same network
docker run -d --name db --network myapp-network -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=secret postgres:16-alpine

docker run -d --name api --network myapp-network -p 3000:3000 myapp:1.0.0

# The API can reach the database by name:
# DATABASE_URL=postgres://postgres:secret@db:5432/mydb

# Verify connectivity:
docker exec api ping db
PING db (172.18.0.2) 56(84) bytes of data.
64 bytes from db.myapp-network (172.18.0.2): icmp_seq=1 ttl=64 time=0.089ms

items.label: Exposing Ports — Best Practices
items.code:
# BAD: Expose database to the world
docker run -d -p 5432:5432 --name db postgres:16-alpine
# Anyone on your network can connect to port 5432

# GOOD: Expose only what needs external access
# Bind database to localhost only (for local tooling)
docker run -d -p 127.0.0.1:5432:5432 --name db postgres:16-alpine

# BEST: Don't expose the database port at all
# API connects via the internal Docker network
docker network create myapp-network
docker run -d --name db --network myapp-network postgres:16-alpine
docker run -d --name api --network myapp-network -p 3000:3000 myapp
# Database is only accessible from other containers on the same network

items.label: Network Isolation — Internal Networks
items.code:
# Create an internal network (no external internet access)
docker network create --internal backend-network

# Database on internal network cannot reach the internet
docker run -d --name db --network backend-network postgres:16-alpine

# Create a regular network for frontend
docker network create frontend-network

# API connects to both: frontend (for web traffic) and backend (for database)
docker run -d --name api \
    --network frontend-network \
    -p 3000:3000 \
    myapp:1.0.0

# Add API to backend network too
docker network connect backend-network api

# Now:
# - Web traffic reaches API via frontend-network
# - API reaches database via backend-network
# - Database has no internet access (internal network)

sections.title: 11. Docker Compose — Multi-Container Applications
sections.description: Defining and running multi-service applications with Docker Compose.
content: Docker Compose lets you define your entire application stack — multiple services, networks, and volumes — in a single YAML file. With one command (docker compose up), you start everything: the web server, API, database, cache, and any other service.

In 2026, Docker Compose v2 is the standard. The legacy docker-compose (Python, hyphenated) command is fully retired. Compose files use the unified Compose Specification, and the version: top-level key is deprecated.

infoBox.content: The canonical filename is now compose.yaml (though docker-compose.yml still works). The version: key is deprecated — Compose v2.21+ ignores it entirely. The schema is implicit based on the file content.

items.label: Compose File Structure
items.code:
# compose.yaml — Basic web app with PostgreSQL
name: myapp

services:
  web:
    build: .
    ports:
      - "3000:3000"
    environment:
      - DATABASE_URL=postgres://myapp:secret@db:5432/mydb
      - NODE_ENV=production
    depends_on:
      db:
        condition: service_healthy
    networks:
      - frontend
      - backend
    restart: unless-stopped

  db:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    volumes:
      - pg_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    environment:
      - POSTGRES_USER=myapp
      - POSTGRES_PASSWORD=secret
      - POSTGRES_DB=mydb
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U myapp -d mydb"]
      interval: 10s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5
    networks:
      - backend
    restart: unless-stopped

networks:
  frontend:
  backend:
    internal: true

volumes:
  pg_data:

rows: Start all services in detached mode
Follow logs from a specific service
Execute command in a running service
Build or rebuild all service images
Validate and view the resolved compose file

items.label: Start Your Stack
items.code:
user@host:~/myapp$ docker compose up -d
[+] Running 4/4
 ✔ Network myapp_frontend    Created
 ✔ Network myapp_backend     Created
 ✔ Volume myapp_pg_data      Created
 ✔ Container myapp-web-1     Started
 ✔ Container myapp-db-1      Started

user@host:~/myapp$ docker compose ps
NAME          IMAGE               COMMAND                  SERVICE   STATUS        PORTS
myapp-web-1   myapp-web           "node server.js"         web       running       0.0.0.0:3000->3000/tcp
myapp-db-1    postgres:16-alpine  "docker-entrypoint.…"    db        running (healthy)  5432/tcp

user@host:~/myapp$ docker compose logs -f web
myapp-web-1  | Server listening on port 3000
myapp-web-1  | Connected to database at db:5432

user@host:~/myapp$ curl http://localhost:3000/health
{"status":"ok","database":"connected"}

user@host:~/myapp$ docker compose down
[+] Running 3/3
 ✔ Container myapp-web-1     Removed
 ✔ Container myapp-db-1      Removed
 ✔ Network myapp_frontend    Removed
 ✔ Network myapp_backend     Removed
# Volume myapp_pg_data persists (not removed with down)

items.label: Docker Compose for Development (Hot Reload)
items.code:
# compose.dev.yaml — Development overrides
name: myapp-dev

services:
  web:
    build:
      context: .
      dockerfile: Dockerfile.dev
    volumes:
      - .:/app          # Bind mount for live code reload
    ports:
      - "3000:3000"
      - "9229:9229"    # Debug port
    environment:
      - NODE_ENV=development
      - DEBUG=true
    command: npx nodemon --inspect=0.0.0.0 server.js

  db:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    ports:
      - "5432:5432"     # Expose for local DB tools
    volumes:
      - pg_data_dev:/var/lib/postgresql/data
      - ./init.sql:/docker-entrypoint-initdb.d/init.sql
      environment:
      - POSTGRES_USER=myapp
      - POSTGRES_PASSWORD=secret
      - POSTGRES_DB=mydb

volumes:
  pg_data_dev:

# Usage:
# docker compose -f compose.dev.yaml up -d
# docker compose -f compose.dev.yaml down

sections.title: 12. Docker Compose for Production
sections.description: Production-hardening your Compose stack with health checks, resource limits, secrets, and proper networking.
content: Docker Compose is not just for development. With careful configuration, it is a reliable deployment tool for single-server production environments. The key differences between a dev Compose file and a production one: explicit everything.

items.label: Production Compose File Template
items.code:
# compose.prod.yaml — Production-ready stack
name: myapp-prod

services:
  web:
    image: ghcr.io/myorg/myapp:1.4.2          # Pinned — never :latest in production
    ports:
      - "127.0.0.1:3000:3000"                  # Bind to localhost; reverse proxy in front
    env_file: .env.production
    secrets:
      - db_password
    depends_on:
      db:
        condition: service_healthy
    networks:
      - frontend
      - backend
    restart: unless-stopped
    logging:
      driver: journald
      options:
        tag: "myapp-web"
    deploy:
      resources:
        limits:
          cpus: "1.0"
          memory: 512M
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "wget", "-qO-", "http://localhost:3000/healthz"]
      interval: 30s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 3
      start_period: 10s
    security_opt:
      - no-new-privileges:true
    read_only: true
    tmpfs:
      - /tmp:size=100m,noexec

  db:
    image: postgres:16.3-alpine
    volumes:
      - pg_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    env_file: .env.production
    secrets:
      - db_password
    networks:
      - backend                          # Internal network only
    restart: unless-stopped
    logging:
      driver: journald
      options:
        tag: "myapp-db"
    deploy:
      resources:
        limits:
          cpus: "1.0"
          memory: 512M
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U $$POSTGRES_USER -d $$POSTGRES_DB"]
      interval: 10s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5
    security_opt:
      - no-new-privileges:true

  nginx:
    image: nginx:1.27-alpine
    ports:
      - "80:80"
      - "443:443"
    volumes:
      - ./nginx.conf:/etc/nginx/nginx.conf:ro
      - ./ssl:/etc/nginx/ssl:ro
      - ./public:/var/www/html:ro
    depends_on:
      - web
    networks:
      - frontend
    restart: unless-stopped
    deploy:
      resources:
        limits:
          cpus: "0.5"
          memory: 256M

networks:
  frontend:
  backend:
    internal: true                       # No external internet access

volumes:
  pg_data:
    driver: local

secrets:
  db_password:
    file: ./secrets/db_password.txt

rows: Auto-restart on crash or host reboot
Without this, a service failure requires manual intervention
Prevents app from starting before DB is ready to accept connections
Define what 'healthy' means for each service
Prevents one service from starving others
Provides writable /tmp without compromising read-only FS
DB and internal services not exposed on network

items.label: Environment Variables and Secrets
content: In production, never hardcode secrets in Compose files. Use these methods in order of preference:
1. Docker secrets — files mounted at /run/secrets/ (available in Swarm mode or via the secrets top-level key with file: source)
2. Env file — .env.production (not committed to version control)
3. External secret manager — HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager (via entrypoint script)
items.code:
# Method 1: Docker Secrets (best for passwords, tokens)
services:
  web:
    secrets:
      - db_password
      - api_key
    environment:
      - DB_PASSWORD_FILE=/run/secrets/db_password

secrets:
  db_password:
    file: ./secrets/db_password.txt
  api_key:
    file: ./secrets/api_key.txt

# Method 2: Env file (good for non-secret config)
services:
  web:
    env_file: .env.production

# .env.production file:
# NODE_ENV=production
# DATABASE_URL=postgres://myapp@db:5432/mydb
# REDIS_URL=redis://cache:6379

# Method 3: Environment variables inline (for non-sensitive config only)
services:
  web:
    environment:
      - NODE_ENV=production
      - LOG_LEVEL=info

sections.title: 13. Docker Security Hardening
sections.description: Hardening containers from Dockerfile to runtime.
content: Container security is not automatic. Default Docker settings prioritize ease of use over security. In production, you must harden every layer: the Dockerfile, the Compose configuration, and the runtime environment.

The most critical security practices: run as a non-root user, use read-only filesystems, drop unnecessary capabilities, never mount the Docker socket into containers, and scan images for vulnerabilities.

infoBox.content: Never mount /var/run/docker.sock into a container unless you fully understand the implications. It gives the container root-level access to the Docker daemon — effectively root on the host.

rows: Container runs as root — if compromised, attacker has host root
Attacker can write malicious files, modify application code
cap_drop: ALL + cap_add only what's needed
Unnecessary kernel capabilities expand attack surface
Container has unrestricted access to host devices
Container can execute docker commands as root on host
DoS attack can consume all host memory/CPU
docker scout or Trivy in CI
Unpredictable base image changes introduce CVEs
Database accessible from compromised frontend container
Docker secrets or env_file, not inline env
Secrets visible in docker inspect, Compose files, logs

items.label: Non-Root User in Dockerfile
items.code:
# Dockerfile — always run as non-root
FROM node:22-alpine

# Create a non-root user
RUN addgroup -S appgroup && adduser -S appuser -G appgroup

WORKDIR /app

# Copy and install dependencies (as root — only needed during build)
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm ci --only=production

COPY . .

# Switch to non-root user before CMD
USER appuser

EXPOSE 3000

CMD ["node", "server.js"]

# Verify:
user@host:~$ docker exec myapp whoami
appuser

items.label: Read-Only Filesystem with tmpfs
items.code:
# compose.yaml — production security settings
services:
  app:
    image: myapp:1.0.0
    read_only: true                         # Root filesystem is read-only
    tmpfs:
      - /tmp:size=100m,noexec               # Writable /tmp, no code execution
      - /var/run:size=50m                   # Runtime files
    cap_drop:
      - ALL                                 # Drop all Linux capabilities
    cap_add:
      - NET_BIND_SERVICE                    # Only allow binding to ports < 1024
    security_opt:
      - no-new-privileges:true              # Prevent privilege escalation
    user: "1001:1001"                       # Run as UID:GID (not by name)
    ulimits:
      nofile:
        soft: 1024
        hard: 2048

items.label: Never Use Privileged Mode
items.code:
# BAD — Never do this in production
docker run --privileged myapp

# What --privileged does:
# - Access to all host devices (/dev)
# - Ability to load kernel modules
# - Bypass all capability restrictions
# - Access to host kernel parameters
# In short: the container has almost the same access as root on the host

# GOOD — Be explicit about what you need
# If you need specific device access:
docker run --device /dev/snd:/dev/snd myapp

# If you need specific capabilities:
docker run --cap-add=NET_ADMIN --cap-add=SYS_TIME myapp

sections.title: 14. Image Scanning and Registries
sections.description: Scanning images for vulnerabilities and managing registries.
content: Container images inherit vulnerabilities from their base OS packages, language runtime, and application dependencies. An unpatched CVE in a base image affects every service built on it. Scan images at build time (in CI/CD), at push time (registry-side), and continuously for running images.
In 2026, the primary tools are Docker Scout (built into Docker) and Trivy (open-source, fast, CI-friendly).

items.label: Scan with Docker Scout
items.code:
# Enable Docker Scout (requires Docker Desktop or Docker Hub login)
docker scout quickview myapp:1.0.0
    ✓ Image stored for analysis
    ✓ SBOM loaded for myapp:1.0.0
    ✓ No vulnerable packages detected

# Detailed CVE report
docker scout cves myapp:1.0.0

# Get remediation recommendations
docker scout recommendations myapp:1.0.0

# Scan during build
docker build --provenance=true --sbom=true -t myapp:1.0.0 .
docker scout policy myapp:1.0.0

items.label: Scan with Trivy (Recommended for CI)
items.code:
# Install Trivy
user@host:~$ sudo apt-get install -y trivy

# Scan an image for HIGH and CRITICAL CVEs
trivy image --severity HIGH,CRITICAL --exit-code 1 myapp:1.0.0

myapp:1.0.0 (alpine 3.20.0)
========================================
Total: 0 (HIGH: 0, CRITICAL: 0)

# Scan with more detail
trivy image --format table myapp:1.0.0

# Scan Dockerfile for misconfigurations
trivy config --severity HIGH,CRITICAL Dockerfile

# Generate SARIF output for GitHub Security tab
trivy image --format sarif --output result.sarif myapp:1.0.0

items.label: Tagging and Pushing to a Registry
items.code:
# Log in to a registry
docker login ghcr.io -u myusername
Password:
Login Succeeded

# Tag your image with the registry path
docker tag myapp:1.0.0 ghcr.io/myorg/myapp:1.0.0
docker tag myapp:1.0.0 ghcr.io/myorg/myapp:latest

# Push to the registry
docker push ghcr.io/myorg/myapp:1.0.0
The push refers to repository [ghcr.io/myorg/myapp]
1.0.0: digest: sha256:abc123... size: 1786

docker push ghcr.io/myorg/myapp:latest

# Pull from any machine
docker pull ghcr.io/myorg/myapp:1.0.0

rows: Latest patch for a minor version
Latest minor for a major version
Convenience only — never use in production

sections.title: 15. Logging and Monitoring
sections.description: Managing container logs and monitoring resource usage.
content: Docker provides built-in log management through log drivers and resource monitoring through docker stats. For production, you'll want to use structured logging drivers and integrate with external monitoring systems.

items.label: Configuring Log Drivers
items.code:
# Global config: /etc/docker/daemon.json
{
  "log-driver": "journald",
  "log-opts": {
    "tag": "{{.Name}}/{{.ID}}"
  }
}

# Per-container (or in Compose):
docker run -d \
    --log-driver journald \
    --log-opt tag="myapp-web" \
    --name myapp \
    myapp:1.0.0

# View logs via journalctl:
journalctl -t myapp-web -f

# Compose service:
services:
  web:
    logging:
      driver: journald
      options:
        tag: "myapp-web"

items.label: Monitoring Resource Usage
items.code:
# Real-time container resource usage
docker stats
CONTAINER ID   NAME         CPU %     MEM USAGE / LIMIT     MEM %     NET I/O          BLOCK I/O
abc12345def6   myapp-web    2.15%     128.5MiB / 512MiB     25.1%     1.2MB / 3.4MB    0B / 0B
def67890abc1   myapp-db     5.30%     245.8MiB / 512MiB     48.0%     3.4MB / 1.2MB    45MB / 12MB

# Inspect resource usage for a specific container
docker stats myapp-web --no-stream

# View container events in real time
docker events --filter 'type=container' --filter 'event=die'
2026-07-06T10:30:00Z container die abc12345def6 (name=myapp-web, exitCode=0, image=myapp:1.0.0)

# Check Docker daemon disk usage
docker system df
TYPE            TOTAL     ACTIVE    SIZE      RECLAIMABLE
Images          5         2         1.234GB   856MB (69%)
Containers      3         2         32.5kB    0B (0%)
Local Volumes   2         1         1.5GB     0B (0%)
Build Cache     12        0         245MB     245MB

items.label: Limit Log File Size (json-file driver)
items.code:
# In production, always set log rotation limits:

# /etc/docker/daemon.json
{
  "log-driver": "json-file",
  "log-opts": {
    "max-size": "10m",
    "max-file": "3"
  }
}

# Or per-container:
docker run -d \
    --log-opt max-size=10m \
    --log-opt max-file=3 \
    --name myapp \
    myapp:1.0.0

# In Compose:
services:
  web:
    logging:
      driver: json-file
      options:
        max-size: "10m"
        max-file: "3"

sections.title: 16. Docker in CI/CD Pipelines
sections.description: Integrating Docker builds and scanning into your CI/CD workflow.
content: The standard CI/CD pattern for Docker: build and scan the image, push to a registry, then deploy. Every stage should be automated and gated by quality checks — vulnerability scans, image size limits, and policy compliance.

items.label: GitHub Actions — Docker Build and Push
items.code:
# .github/workflows/docker-build.yml
name: Build and Push Docker Image

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    tags: ['v*']

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: read
      packages: write

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Docker Buildx
        uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v3

      - name: Log in to GitHub Container Registry
        uses: docker/login-action@v3
        with:
          registry: ghcr.io
          username: ${{ github.actor }}
          password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

      - name: Extract metadata
        id: meta
        uses: docker/metadata-action@v5
        with:
          images: ghcr.io/${{ github.repository }}
          tags: |
            type=semver,pattern={{version}}
            type=semver,pattern={{major}}.{{minor}}
            type=sha,format=short
            type=raw,value=latest,enable={{is_default_branch}}

      - name: Build and push
        uses: docker/build-push-action@v6
        with:
          context: .
          push: true
          tags: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.tags }}
          labels: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.labels }}
          cache-from: type=gha
          cache-to: type=gha,mode=max
          provenance: true
          sbom: true

      - name: Scan image with Trivy
        uses: aquasecurity/trivy-action@master
        with:
          image-ref: ghcr.io/${{ github.repository }}:${{ github.sha }}
          format: sarif
          output: trivy-results.sarif
          exit-code: '1'
          severity: 'HIGH,CRITICAL'

      - name: Upload Trivy results
        uses: github/codeql-action/upload-sarif@v3
        with:
          sarif_file: trivy-results.sarif

items.label: GitLab CI — Docker Build and Scan
items.code:
# .gitlab-ci.yml
docker-build:
  stage: build
  image: docker:29
  services:
    - docker:dind
  variables:
    IMAGE_TAG: $CI_REGISTRY_IMAGE:$CI_COMMIT_SHORT_SHA

  before_script:
    - docker login -u $CI_REGISTRY_USER -p $CI_REGISTRY_PASSWORD $CI_REGISTRY
    - apk add --no-cache trivy

  script:
    - docker build --provenance=true --sbom=true -t $IMAGE_TAG .
    - docker tag $IMAGE_TAG $CI_REGISTRY_IMAGE:latest
    - trivy image --severity HIGH,CRITICAL --exit-code 1 $IMAGE_TAG
    - docker push $IMAGE_TAG
    - docker push $CI_REGISTRY_IMAGE:latest

  only:
    - main

sections.title: 17. Docker Best Practices — The Complete Guide
sections.description: Battle-tested patterns for Dockerfiles, Compose, and daily operations.
content: These are the patterns used by teams running Docker in production at scale. Apply them consistently for secure, efficient, and maintainable containerization.

cards.title: Dockerfile Best Practices
cards.desc: Use specific base image tags, multi-stage builds, order layers by change frequency, combine RUN statements, use .dockerignore, run as non-root, set HEALTHCHECK, use BuildKit cache mounts.

cards.title: Docker Compose Best Practices
cards.desc: Pin image versions, use health checks with depends_on condition, set resource limits, use internal networks for DBs, bind ports to localhost, use secrets for sensitive data, set restart policy.

items.label: Dockerfile Best Practices Summary
items.code:
# 1. Pin base image versions with full digest
FROM node:22-alpine@sha256:abc123def456...

# 2. Use multi-stage builds
FROM ... AS builder
# ... build stage
FROM ... AS runtime
COPY --from=builder ...

# 3. Order layers by change frequency (least-changing first)
FROM node:22-alpine
WORKDIR /app                    # Rarely changes
COPY package*.json ./           # Changes with dependencies
RUN npm ci                      # Expensive — cache it
COPY . .                        # Changes most often — keep last

# 4. Combine RUN statements to reduce layers
RUN apt-get update && \
    apt-get install -y pkg1 pkg2 && \
    rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# 5. Use BuildKit cache mounts for package managers
RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/root/.cache/pip \
    pip install -r requirements.txt

# 6. Run as non-root
RUN addgroup -S appgroup && adduser -S appuser -G appgroup
USER appuser

# 7. Add a HEALTHCHECK
HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=3s --retries=3 \
  CMD wget -qO- http://localhost:3000/healthz || exit 1

# 8. Use LABEL for metadata
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.source="https://github.com/myorg/myapp"
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.version="1.4.2"

items.label: Docker Compose Best Practices Summary
items.code:
# compose.prod.yaml — battle-tested production config
name: myapp

services:
  app:
    image: ghcr.io/myorg/myapp:1.4.2          # Pinned version
    ports:
      - "127.0.0.1:3000:3000"                  # Localhost-only binding
    env_file: .env.production
    secrets:
      - db_password
    depends_on:
      db:
        condition: service_healthy             # Wait for readiness
    restart: unless-stopped                    # Auto-recovery
    deploy:
      resources:
        limits:
          cpus: "1.0"
          memory: 512M
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "wget", "-qO-", "http://localhost:3000/healthz"]
      interval: 30s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 3
      start_period: 10s
    read_only: true
    tmpfs:
      - /tmp:size=100m,noexec
    cap_drop:
      - ALL
    cap_add:
      - NET_BIND_SERVICE
    security_opt:
      - no-new-privileges:true
    networks:
      - frontend
      - backend

  db:
    image: postgres:16.3-alpine
    volumes:
      - pg_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    env_file: .env.production
    secrets:
      - db_password
    restart: unless-stopped
    deploy:
      resources:
        limits:
          cpus: "1.0"
          memory: 512M
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U $$POSTGRES_USER -d $$POSTGRES_DB"]
      interval: 10s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5
    networks:
      - backend                                    # Internal network

networks:
  frontend:
  backend:
    internal: true

volumes:
  pg_data:

secrets:
  db_password:
    file: ./secrets/db_password.txt

items.label: Docker Workflow Best Practices
text: 1. Never use :latest in production — always pin exact versions with semantic tags
2. Use .dockerignore to exclude unnecessary files from build context
3. Set log rotation limits (max-size: 10m, max-file: 3) to prevent disk filling
4. Use docker system prune periodically to clean unused resources
5. Never run containers as root (unless absolutely required)
6. Never mount the Docker socket into a container
7. Scan images for CVEs before every deployment
8. Use BuildKit for faster builds (default in Docker 29+)
9. Separate Compose files for dev and production (compose.dev.yaml, compose.prod.yaml)
10. Always set resource limits to prevent noisy neighbor problems

sections.title: 18. Troubleshooting Common Issues
sections.description: Solutions to the most frequent Docker problems.

items.label: Issue 1: Port Already Allocated
items.code:
docker: Error response from daemon: driver failed programming external connectivity
on endpoint myapp (abc123): Bind for 0.0.0.0:3000 failed: port is already allocated.

# Find what's using the port:
user@host:~$ docker ps --format '{{.Names}} {{.Ports}}' | grep 3000
my-other-app 0.0.0.0:3000->3000/tcp

# Stop the conflicting container:
user@host:~$ docker stop my-other-app

# Or use a different port:
user@host:~$ docker run -d -p 3001:3000 --name myapp myapp:1.0.0

items.label: Issue 2: Connection Refused Between Containers
items.code:
# Error: API cannot connect to database: connect ECONNREFUSED db:5432

# Check if containers are on the same network:
user@host:~$ docker inspect api --format '{{range $net, $v := .NetworkSettings.Networks}}{{$net}}{{end}}'
bridge

user@host:~$ docker inspect db --format '{{range $net, $v := .NetworkSettings.Networks}}{{$net}}{{end}}'
myapp_default

# Fix: Put both on the same user-defined network:
user@host:~$ docker network create myapp-network
user@host:~$ docker network connect myapp-network api
user@host:~$ docker network connect myapp-network db

# Verify DNS resolution:
user@host:~$ docker exec api ping db
PING db (172.18.0.2) 56(84) bytes of data.
64 bytes from db.myapp-network (172.18.0.2): icmp_seq=1 ttl=64 time=0.089ms

items.label: Issue 3: Docker Disk Space Full
items.code:
# Check disk usage:
user@host:~$ docker system df
TYPE            TOTAL     ACTIVE    SIZE      RECLAIMABLE
Images          15        3         5.2GB     3.8GB (73%)
Containers      5         2         1.2MB     0B (0%)
Local Volumes   4         1         8.5GB     7.2GB (85%)
Build Cache     28        0         1.8GB     1.8GB

# Clean up:
user@host:~$ docker system prune -a --volumes
WARNING! This will remove:
  - all stopped containers
  - all networks not used by at least one container
  - all volumes not used by at least one container
  - all dangling images
  - all build cache
Are you sure you want to continue? [y/N] y

Total reclaimed space: 14.3GB

items.label: Issue 4: Container Exits Immediately
items.code:
# Problem: container runs briefly then exits with code 0 or 1

# Check logs:
user@host:~$ docker logs myapp
> myapp@1.0.0 start
> node server.js

Server listening on port 3000
# But then exits? That's a process manager issue.

# Fix: Ensure your CMD runs as a foreground process, not a background one:
# BAD: CMD service nginx start    (starts in background, container exits)
# GOOD: CMD nginx -g 'daemon off;'  (runs in foreground)

# Check exit code:
user@host:~$ docker inspect myapp --format '{{.State.ExitCode}}'
1

# Common causes:
# - Exit code 0: CMD finished, no process kept alive
# - Exit code 1: Application crashed during startup
# - Exit code 137: OOM killed (received SIGKILL)
# - Exit code 139: Segmentation fault

items.label: Issue 5: Permission Denied on Bind Mount
items.code:
# Error: Permission denied when trying to write to a bind-mounted directory

# The container user (usually root or node) doesn't match the host file ownership

# Solutions:

# 1. Create the directory with proper permissions before mounting:
user@host:~$ mkdir -p ./data && sudo chown 1001:1001 ./data

# 2. Use a named volume instead of a bind mount:
docker run -d -v app_data:/app/data myapp

# 3. In development, run as the current host user:
docker run -d -u $(id -u):$(id -g) -v $(pwd)/data:/app/data myapp

# 4. Use Docker's rootless mode (advanced, no permission issues)

items.label: Issue 6: docker compose down -v Deleted My Database

infoBox.content: docker compose down -v removes ALL volumes. This is destructive and irreversible unless you have backups. Never run this command in production without verifying you have a recent database backup.

items.text:
The -v flag in docker compose down removes named volumes declared in the Compose file. This is the #1 cause of accidental data loss in Docker.

Safe alternatives:
- docker compose down (without -v) — stops containers, removes networks, preserves volumes
- docker compose stop — just stops containers, everything else stays
- docker compose pause — freezes containers without stopping them

To recover from accidental volume deletion: restore from your last backup (you do have backups, right?).

sections.title: 19. Summary and Next Steps
sections.description: Key takeaways and resources for continuing your Docker journey.

items.label: What You Learned
text: ✓ Docker concepts — images vs containers, Docker Engine, registries, layers
✓ Running containers — lifecycle management, port mapping, exec, logs
✓ Dockerfile writing — FROM, RUN, COPY, CMD, ENV, EXPOSE, USER, HEALTHCHECK
✓ Layer optimization — ordering layers by change frequency, BuildKit cache mounts
✓ Multi-stage builds — separating build and runtime for smaller, more secure images
✓ Volumes and persistence — named volumes, bind mounts, tmpfs, backup strategies
✓ Docker networking — bridge, host, user-defined networks, DNS, network isolation
✓ Docker Compose — multi-service stacks, compose.yaml, development vs production configs
✓ Security hardening — non-root user, read-only FS, cap_drop, secrets management
✓ Image scanning — Trivy, Docker Scout, CI/CD integration for vulnerability detection
✓ Production patterns — health checks, resource limits, restart policies, logging

items.label: Key Commands Reference
items.code:
# ── Image Management ──────────────────────────────────────
docker images                         # List images
docker build -t myapp:tag .            # Build an image
docker pull nginx:alpine               # Pull from registry
docker push myuser/myapp:tag           # Push to registry
docker rmi IMAGE                       # Remove image
docker tag SOURCE TARGET               # Tag an image

# ── Container Management ───────────────────────────────────
docker run -d -p 8080:80 nginx         # Run detached with port mapping
docker ps -a                           # List all containers
docker stop CONTAINER                  # Stop a container
docker rm CONTAINER                    # Remove a container
docker logs -f CONTAINER               # Follow logs
docker exec -it CONTAINER sh           # Interactive shell inside container
docker inspect CONTAINER               # Detailed metadata

# ── Volumes and Networks ───────────────────────────────────
docker volume create NAME              # Create volume
docker volume ls                       # List volumes
docker network create NAME             # Create network
docker network ls                      # List networks
docker network connect NET CONTAINER   # Connect container to network

# ── Docker Compose ──────────────────────────────────────────
docker compose up -d                   # Start all services
docker compose down                    # Stop and remove
docker compose logs -f                 # Follow all logs
docker compose exec SERVICE sh         # Shell into service
docker compose ps                      # Service status

# ── Cleanup ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
docker system prune                    # Remove unused data
docker system prune -a --volumes       # Aggressive cleanup (DESTRUCTIVE)
docker container prune                 # Remove stopped containers
docker volume prune                    # Remove unused volumes

# ── Debugging ───────────────────────────────────────────────
docker stats                           # Resource usage
docker events                          # Real-time events
docker system df                       # Disk usage
docker inspect CONTAINER | grep IP     # Find container IP

items.label: Recommended Learning Path
text: 1. Docker's official documentation (docs.docker.com) — the single best reference
2. Play with Docker (labs.play-with-docker.com) — free online Docker playground
3. Docker Best Practices Guide on docs.docker.com — official best practices for Dockerfiles and Compose
4. CNCF Cloud Native Interactive Landscape (landscape.cncf.io) — see how Docker fits in the ecosystem
5. Study real-world compose.yaml files from open-source projects on GitHub
6. Practice deploying a multi-service stack with Docker Compose on a VPS
7. Next: learn Kubernetes — it extends Docker concepts to multi-node orchestration

items.label: Quick Reference: Docker Compose Lifecycle
items.code:
# Development workflow:
git clone <repo> && cd <project>
docker compose -f compose.dev.yaml up -d    # Start dev environment
docker compose -f compose.dev.yaml logs -f  # Watch logs
# Make code changes (hot reload via bind mount)
docker compose -f compose.dev.yaml down     # Stop

# Production workflow:
docker compose -f compose.prod.yaml build
docker compose -f compose.prod.yaml up -d
docker compose -f compose.prod.yaml ps
docker compose -f compose.prod.yaml logs -f web
# Update:
docker compose -f compose.prod.yaml pull
docker compose -f compose.prod.yaml up -d --force-recreate
docker compose -f compose.prod.yaml down
